content.text: Token Quota Forecasting for Agentic Workloads

Agentic systems are not stationary in their token consumption — usage grows as more workflows are onboarded, exhibits weekly rhythms, and can spike unpredictably when a new automation goes live. Treating quota as a static monthly number leads to either chronic under-provisioning (rejected requests mid-sprint) or over-spend on idle capacity. A proper forecasting discipline turns historical token telemetry into actionable quota recommendations, advance alerts, and a defensible cost projection for finance.

attrs.alt: Token Quota Forecasting: Moving Average and Seasonality
attrs.title: 7-day weighted moving average with forecast band and weekly seasonality

content.text: Why Token Forecasting Differs From Compute Forecasting
Classic capacity planning targets request throughput or CPU utilisation, both of which scale roughly linearly with load. Token consumption has additional dimensions:
Per-agent variability.
A search agent that retrieves ten chunks produces far more tokens than a classifier that emits a single label. Each agent archetype has its own token signature.
Prompt-length inflation.
As teams enrich system prompts, add few-shot examples, and extend conversation history windows, the token cost per request grows independent of throughput.
Output variability.
Structured JSON output, chain-of-thought reasoning, and streaming completions drive output token counts that are harder to predict than deterministic application code.
Weekly seasonality.
Enterprise deployments consistently show Monday-to-Friday peaks and weekend troughs of 30–60%, with periodic end-of-quarter surges tied to reporting cycles.
Provider-imposed ceilings.
Unlike compute autoscaling that simply spins up new nodes, LLM tokens are bounded by account-level agreements and per-minute rate limits, making over-run a harder failure mode.
Measuring Token Usage Per Agent and Per Workflow
Instrumentation Strategy
Every LLM call must emit a structured telemetry event that captures at minimum the tokens consumed and the agent or workflow that consumed them. The recommended schema:

{
  "timestamp": "2025-06-30T14:23:01Z",
  "agent_id": "search-agent",
  "workflow_id": "weekly-market-report",
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-5",
  "prompt_tokens": 3_420,
  "completion_tokens": 612,
  "total_tokens": 4_032,
  "request_latency_ms": 1_840,
  "cost_usd": 0.0241
}

In practice, wrap your LLM client in a thin instrumentation layer that intercepts the usage object returned in every response and publishes it to your observability backend (DataDog, OpenTelemetry Collector, or a simple ClickHouse table).
Aggregation Dimensions
Roll up raw events into daily grain by:
Agent ID — understand which automation is the heaviest consumer.
Workflow type — separate batch pipelines from interactive copilots; they have different growth trajectories.
Model tier — cheaper models may be substituted for specific tasks without quality regression, so tracking by model enables optimisation.
Environment — production vs staging vs load-test to avoid skewing forecasts.
Forecasting Methodology
Weighted Moving Average

A 7-day weighted moving average (WMA) is the minimum viable forecaster for most teams. It is robust to single-day anomalies, responds quickly to genuine trend shifts, and requires no model fitting infrastructure. More recent days receive higher weights, so a sudden ramp-up in usage propagates into the forecast within a week.
Seasonality Correction

Compute a day-of-week multiplier from at least four historical weeks: divide each weekday's average tokens by the overall daily mean. Apply this multiplier to the trend baseline to produce seasonally adjusted daily forecasts. This single adjustment captures the majority of predictable variation in enterprise workloads.
Python Token Usage Forecaster
The forecaster's
quota_recommendation()
method applies a 25% safety factor on top of the predicted total. Adjust this factor based on your organisation's risk appetite and whether your provider offers burst capacity you can draw on.
Quota Allocation Strategy Across Teams
Allocation Principles
Allocate quota to teams using a three-tier model:
Base allocation
— the 90th-percentile historical consumption of each team over the past three months, rounded up to the nearest 500 K tokens.
Growth buffer
— add a percentage equal to the team's month-over-month growth rate × 1.5 to accommodate adoption curves.
Global safety reserve
— hold back 10% of total purchased tokens to absorb cross-team surprises; make this available as an on-demand burst pool.
YAML Quota Policy

# quota-policy.yaml  — per-team token quota allocation
version: "1"
period: "monthly"

global_budget:
  total_tokens: 50_000_000        # hard ceiling from provider agreement
  safety_reserve_pct: 10          # held back for burst headroom
  burst_pool_tokens: 2_000_000    # drawn on by teams when their quota is exhausted

teams:
  - id: search-agent
    display_name: "Search & Retrieval Agent"
    allocated_tokens: 15_000_000
    daily_limit: 600_000
    alert_threshold_pct: 80       # alert ops at 80% of monthly allocation
    on_exhaustion: "queue"        # queue | reject | burst-pool

  - id: summariser
    display_name: "Document Summarisation Pipeline"
    allocated_tokens: 10_000_000
    daily_limit: 400_000
    alert_threshold_pct: 75
    on_exhaustion: "burst-pool"

  - id: code-review
    display_name: "Automated Code Review Agent"
    allocated_tokens: 8_000_000
    daily_limit: 300_000
    alert_threshold_pct: 85
    on_exhaustion: "reject"       # SLA permits rejection for non-critical work

  - id: customer-support
    display_name: "Customer Support Copilot"
    allocated_tokens: 12_000_000
    daily_limit: 450_000
    alert_threshold_pct: 70
    on_exhaustion: "burst-pool"

exhaustion_policy:
  queue:
    max_queue_depth: 500
    max_wait_seconds: 120
    overflow: "reject"
  burst_pool:
    max_draw_per_team_pct: 25     # a team may draw at most 25% of burst pool
    replenishment: "next_billing_cycle"

notifications:
  channels: [slack, pagerduty]
  events: [threshold_crossed, daily_limit_hit, quota_exhausted, burst_pool_low]
On-Exhaustion Policies
Three strategies apply when a team hits its limit before the billing period resets:
queue
— buffer requests in a priority queue, honouring SLAs for high-priority workflows while deferring background tasks.
burst-pool
— draw from the shared reserve without explicit per-team approval; suitable for customer-facing workloads where latency matters.
reject
— return an HTTP 429 to the caller immediately; appropriate for batch workloads with natural retry logic or for non-critical automation that can safely pause.
Budget Exhaustion Handling
Detection and Alerting

Alert at 70% of monthly allocation (seven to nine days warning), 85%, and 95%. The 85% alert triggers an ops review; the 95% alert triggers an automated quota top-up request to your LLM provider. Each alert must include the projected exhaustion date computed from the current daily burn rate so that responders can gauge urgency immediately.
Graceful Degradation Patterns
Model downgrade
— route non-latency-sensitive requests to a cheaper, smaller model that counts against a separate, cheaper quota.
Prompt compression
— engage a pre-compression step that strips verbose context from prompts when the daily limit is within 20% of exhaustion.
Workflow prioritisation
— implement a priority enum on every workflow request (CRITICAL / HIGH / NORMAL / BATCH) and shed BATCH-class requests first.
Human escalation
— for customer-facing agents nearing exhaustion, hand off to a human support queue rather than failing silently.
Cost Projection Models
Simple Per-Token Pricing Model
Given a forecast of total monthly tokens, compute projected cost as:

def project_monthly_cost(
    forecast_tokens: int,
    prompt_fraction: float,       # fraction of tokens that are prompt tokens
    input_price_per_mtok: float,  # USD per million input tokens
    output_price_per_mtok: float, # USD per million output tokens
) -> dict:
    input_tokens = forecast_tokens * prompt_fraction
    output_tokens = forecast_tokens * (1 - prompt_fraction)
    cost = (
        input_tokens / 1_000_000 * input_price_per_mtok
        + output_tokens / 1_000_000 * output_price_per_mtok
    )
    return {
        "forecast_tokens": forecast_tokens,
        "input_tokens": int(input_tokens),
        "output_tokens": int(output_tokens),
        "estimated_cost_usd": round(cost, 2),
    }

example = project_monthly_cost(
    forecast_tokens=45_000_000,
    prompt_fraction=0.85,
    input_price_per_mtok=3.0,
    output_price_per_mtok=15.0,
)
print(example)
# {"forecast_tokens": 45000000, "input_tokens": 38250000,
#  "output_tokens": 6750000, "estimated_cost_usd": 216.0}
Scenario Planning
Produce three cost scenarios for each quarter:
Base case
— WMA forecast with historical growth rate.
Optimistic
— base case minus 15%; assumes prompt compression and model-tier optimisation ship on schedule.
Conservative
— base case multiplied by 1.35; accounts for unexpected scope expansion or new team onboarding.
Present all three to finance alongside the technical assumptions behind each so that any stakeholder can recompute the scenario by changing a single parameter.
Operational Playbook
Weekly
: review actual vs forecast variance; recalibrate WMA window if error exceeds 20% for three consecutive days.
Monthly
: re-run seasonality multiplier calculation with the latest four weeks of data.
Quarterly
: renegotiate provider quota tiers based on conservative-scenario projection; request committed-use discounts.
Event-driven
: trigger an immediate re-forecast whenever a new agent archetype is added to production or an existing workflow's context window doubles.
Audit
: retain 90 days of per-agent daily token logs to support SOC 2 inquiries and finance reconciliation.
Summary
Instrument every LLM call with agent ID, workflow type, and token counts to build a reliable historical signal.
A 7-day weighted moving average with day-of-week seasonality handles most enterprise forecasting needs without machine learning infrastructure.
Allocate quota in three tiers: base (p90 historical), growth buffer, and global safety reserve.
Enforce exhaustion policies per team (queue / burst-pool / reject) rather than applying a single global strategy.
Alert at 70%, 85%, and 95% of monthly allocation; include projected exhaustion date in every alert.
Project cost in three scenarios (base / optimistic / conservative) and socialise assumptions with finance quarterly.
